headlines.title: STAPLE TRADE OPENS ACTIVE; DEMAND GOOD
headlines.subtitle: Movement to Inland Points Show Liberal Increase Over Previous Week.
headlines.fullText:
The Jobbing markets opened with a fair volume of business Tuesday. Country merchants are placing larger orders for staples, and the movement to Inland points is considerably in- creased. Shipments are going forward in a greater volume than for several weeks, Prices on staples are firm, but practically no changes are announced.
The cotton crop is expected to be- gin to move more rapidly, and with the combined efforts of banks and mer- chants the farmer will be able to real- ize cash from his cotton as fast as ginned. This, it is believed by business men, will soon place business on a formal basis.

headlines.title: Hogs Big Dime Higher; Cattle Remain Steady
headlines.subtitle: Twenty-Two Carloads of Mexican Steers Sell at $5.45-Best Beeves Make $7.50 Choice Hogs Sell at $9.30 to $9.35.
headlines.fullText:
More than tifty carlosda of Mexican cattle were on the market Tuesday, but prices never varied far from steady with Monday's basis. Twenty-two care loads of Mexican steers sold at $5.45, and one bunch of Mexican steers went to a feeder buyer at $i.d. Mexican calves brought $4.80 to $5. Beat fat steers notched $7.50.
Quotations: Reeves, $5.25 to $7.50; stockers, $5.25 to $5.75; cows, $3.25 to $5.50; heifers, 35.60 to stas: bulls $4.25 to $5.10: calves, 34.50 to #8.
Buyers took care of a supply 1.900 hogs at prices that were gener. ally a big dime higher. one, car of the crop went at sh wgs and bulk of the sales were at $9.30 to $9.35.
Sheep market was active -en decks de gases and strong sold at $5.75 and four decks of weth- ers and ewes made $5.50. Receipts were close to 1,000 head.

headlines.title: WHEAT LOWER; WAR REPORTS HELP TO BEARS
headlines.subtitle: Heavy Selling Follows News of Success of Allied Forces.
headlines.fullText: Setcial to The Star.Teltoram, CHICAGO, Sept. 8 .- Wheat downward today, influenced by reports of big victorien for the abien near Paris and because of talk that pence might come with as surprising suddenness as had characterized the beginning of the war. General selling set in at the start and there was no check to the decline until prices had receded as much as 6% cents in some cases. The greatest setback was in the December delivery, which fall to $1.16, as com- pared with $1.23 a saturday night. L.a. fer December fallled to $1.19. Changes ***. After opening No to 1%e ad- vance, the market underwent an all- round decline, averaging 1c. Oats responded to peace talk almost as readily as wheat did. It was said that export sales were small Grain weakness made Itself felt with decisive effect on provisions. Higher prices for hogs formed only & tem- porary check to the downward move- ment.

headlines.title: IMPROVEMENT INDICATED IN COTTON CROP
headlines.subtitle: Ginning Figures of Only 475,- 455 Bales Show Late- ness of Crop.
headlines.fullText:
The report on the number of bales ginned from the crop of 1914 to Sept. 1, which was 475,455, compared with 771.297 in 1911 and 383.011 in 1910. 18 an indication of the lateness of the crop. It is believed that there is a wonder- tul improvement in the condition of cotton since the last government con- dition report was made, and that this Jear promises a bumper cotton crop. The government weather map shows Higher entends und higher temperatures, and much His- provernent has taken place. The sta- tistical position of cotton is strong. Reports of anet males in the South are on a slightly higher baals. Monday and Turn- der @ considerable amount of cotton Is reported to have been taken in Texas at prices ranging around 60 for mid- diing. However, at these figures no cotton is pressed for sale.
Sea Island Included, 1,578 bales, com- pared with 436 last year; 232 for 1912 and 54s for 1911. Ginning states: Alabama, 46,764; Arkansas, 514; Florida, 6,223; Georgia, 136,079: Louisiana, 8,743; Mississippi, 2,693: North Carolina, 370: Oklahoma, "South Carolina 14,864: Tennes- Bee. 316; Texan, 264,200; all other states, 28.

headlines.title: FALLS FROM MOTORCYCLE; DEATH LIKELY
headlines.subtitle: Clyde Armstrong Sustains Probably Fatal Injuries; Friend Badly Hurt.
headlines.fullText:
Clyde Arrastrong, 23, 2812 Travis avenue, is in the Chs-County noa- pital suffering from. concussion of the brain and various other injuries which areAS causas death as a Arlington Heights read Monday att- ernoon.
John Bozeman, 18, 1301 Johnson street, who was riding a few feet be- hind Armstrong, and co deunutma wheel, in in the hospital with a frac- tured shoulder and other injuries. Ac- count count of the hospital les
"Red" Helmcamp, in a semicon- scious condition from injuries in a mo- torecycle acident on the same road ten days ago. Doctors have about given up hope for Helmenmp's recovery. He Is a close friend of both Armstrong and Bozeman, and participated In Fourth of July run with them.
Armstrong has never regained con- sciousness. Besides the terrible gash on his head, he is suffering with a hattered count mama and broken right ankle. He was already crippled In his left leg. Odds are against his recovery.

headlines.title: CITY SCHOOL DISTRICT LINES ARE CHANGED
headlines.subtitle: Tyra's Successor Will Be Named at October Meeting. Changes in Faculty.
headlines.fullText:
New street lines, marking publie school districts, was prepare districts are announced.Tues day by Superintendent Cantwell as fol- lows:
South Side-Line between George C. Clarke and F. M. Daggett school In below seventh grade attend Clarke School; all other pupils in seventh, high and low, attend Daggett. All other pu- pils south of Myrtle street in high nev. enth grade will attend Daggett. Low seventh grade pupils will attend as usu- al. The line The. ne dividing the new seventh grade district runs from Illinois to Magnolia to the L & G. N. tracks. All pupils above the fifth grade will attend M. Peak School: below the fifth grade pupils will attend schools in their re- spective districts.
North Side The dividing line be- tween Circle Park and Denver Ave- mue schoole will be Twentieth street. The dividing line between M. G. El- His. Denver Avenue and Circle Park schools will be Twentieth street. All pupils east of Twentieth street and west of Lee to Market street, go to Circle Park. All other North Side schools have grades one to aix in their respective districts.
.. The school board has named Miss Mattle Beth Morgan an Instructor in zoology in Central High Nchool, Miss Emma Perdue, teacher of muste in the sixth and elghth course, Central High: Miss Myrtle Me- Peak and Miss Mary Faires, teachers In kindergarten.
The board at its October meeting will name a successor to E. T. Tyra, former president, who resigned Monday. It al- so will fill the vacancy created by his resignation. The board also will name a successor to George H. Clough, Instructor in phys- ics, who resigned to become principal of the Corsicana High School.

headlines.title: REGISTRY OF STUDENTS AT T. C. U. HEAVY
headlines.fullText:
Dining halls at Texas Christian Uni- versity were filled to overflowing Tuesday at lunch, as evidence of the tremendous registry of students in the last twenty-four hours.
With the exception of a few rooms In Price, Hall, the boys' dormitory. tou dormitories were must to capacity Tuesday at noon. The girls dormitory is filled, and truny man girls have signified their intention of coming that they will have to be put in rooms with the others.
Fifty matriculated Tuesday morning, it was announced.
All of the faculty have arrived. Among the late arrivals are Prof. and Mrs. W. H. Batson from Ogborn, Ohio. When Prof. Batson closed the season at T. C. U. last term and went to Ohio he was a single man. Only ten days ago he married Miss Winifred Creamer. Prot. Batnun wan superintendent of public schools at Osborn for several years.

headlines.title: LIVESTOCK SANITARY WORK PRAISED
headlines.fullText:
Strong resolutions Indorsing the work of the state livestock sanitary commission and commending the work of the present members of the commis- sion were adopted by the executive committee of the Cattle Raisers' Asso- ciation of Texas, In session Tuesday morning at the Westbrook hotel.
The resolutions further urge all members of the association to co-op- erate with the state and Federal offi- calm to nie the state of ticks, scables and other livestock diseases.
Favorable action was takes upon 's applications for membership. These applicants represented a total of 45,642 cattle.
The members of the comm , attendance at this Ence ne more Burnett, Fort Worth: F. S. Hastings, Stamford: John McBain, deMad. Cole .: N. H. Corder, Sander- son: R. J. Cook, Beville; L., Ward, San Antonio: H. C. Harding. Amarillo: W. W. Weatheraber, Del Rio; M. Sansom, Fort Worth, and J. M. Bassett, Cron- byton.

headlines.title: HOME BURNED
headlines.subtitle: Family Went to Sleep With Lamp
headlines.fullText:
The home of Arthur T. Tewmey, 2508 South Jones street, was burned at 2:30 B. m. Tuesday, When the family re- tired a lamp was left burning on a ta- ble. That room was in flames when the family awoke. The loss is estimated at $1,700.
Insurance was $1,000 on building and $350 on contents.

headlines.title: Will be Guardians of Convicted Girl
headlines.subtitle: Judge and Lawyer
headlines.fullText: After Lulu Faxton, 17, had pleaded guilty to a charge of trying to paas a forged Instrument, in the Forty. eighth district court Tuesday morning and sentence had been suspended by the fury, Judge R. M. Huck refused to let her go until he could be anpared ther ape sit would have some place to co when she was released. To keep her from drifting into eva way of life, Attorney W. E. Williams, who had been connected with the case. agreed to take her to his home until a place could be found for her. Jucken Hald that he would also keep an eye on the girl, as she would be near his home.
